audit_best_practices.overview: Best practices and guidelines for conducting effective repository audits that provide actionable insights and drive meaningful improvements.

principle.name: Organized Audit Folder Structure
principle.description: Always create a well-organized folder structure for each audit. This ensures all artifacts are properly organized, easy to find, and can coexist with other audits.
principle.rationale: Proper organization makes audit results accessible, supports multiple audits of the same or different Object Stores, and provides clear separation between different types of artifacts (reports, data, analysis, recommendations).
principle.structure: audits/ └── [ObjectStoreName]_[YYYY-MM-DD]/ ├── audit_metadata.json ├── README.md ├── reports/ │ ├── Repository_Audit_Report.md │ └── Executive_Summary.md ├── data/ │ ├── class_inventory.csv │ ├── property_matrix.csv │ ├── document_counts.csv │ └── integration_points.json ├── analysis/ │ ├── class_analysis.md │ ├── property_analysis.md │ └── folder_analysis.md └── recommendations/ ├── consolidation_plans.md ├── cleanup_actions.md └── implementation_roadmap.md

benefits.benefit: Clear separation of concerns (reports vs data vs recommendations)
Easy navigation with README.md as entry point
Multiple audits can coexist without conflicts
Object Store name in folder makes identification easy
Date stamp enables version tracking and comparison
Metadata file provides audit context and history

implementation.step: 1. Identify Object Store name at audit start
2. Create folder structure using execute_command with mkdir -p
4. Generate all artifacts in appropriate subfolders

principle.name: Consistent File Naming
principle.description: Use consistent, descriptive file names across all audits. This makes it easy to understand file contents and compare across different audits.

naming_conventions.convention: - Repository_Audit_Report.md (main comprehensive report) - Executive_Summary.md (high-level summary for stakeholders)

- class_inventory.csv (complete class list with statistics) - property_matrix.csv (property usage across classes) - document_counts.csv (document counts per class) - integration_points.json (integration property mapping)
- class_analysis.md (detailed class findings) - property_analysis.md (property usage and issues) - folder_analysis.md (folder structure assessment)
- consolidation_plans.md (class consolidation strategies) - cleanup_actions.md (cleanup tasks and priorities) - implementation_roadmap.md (phased implementation plan)

principle.name: Audit Metadata Tracking
principle.description: Maintain audit_metadata.json to track audit context, status, and history.

principle.name: Systematic and Methodical Approach
principle.description: Follow the audit workflow phases in order. Complete each phase before moving to the next. This ensures comprehensive coverage and prevents missing critical information.
principle.rationale: Repository audits involve complex, interconnected data. A systematic approach ensures nothing is overlooked and findings are properly contextualized.

example.good: 1. List all root classes 2. For each class, get properties 3. For each class, count documents 4. Analyze patterns across all classes 5. Generate consolidated report
example.bad: Jumping between different classes randomly, analyzing some properties but not others, generating partial reports without complete data.

principle.name: Document as You Go
principle.description: Create the audit report file at the start and continuously update it with findings. Don't wait until the end to document everything.
principle.rationale: Audits generate large amounts of data. Documenting incrementally prevents information loss and makes it easier to spot patterns as they emerge.

example.good: After analyzing each class, immediately add a section to the report with: - Class name and description - Property count and types - Document count - Initial observations
example.bad: Analyzing all classes first, then trying to remember and document everything at the end.

principle.name: Use Concrete Examples
principle.description: When identifying issues or patterns, always provide specific examples with actual class names, property names, and data.
principle.rationale: Concrete examples make findings actionable and help stakeholders understand the issues. Generic statements are less useful for driving change.

example.good:
"Found 4 duplicate client document classes: - ClientDocument (150 docs) - usr1_Client_Document (23 docs) - usr2_Client_document (8 docs) - welClientDocument (45 docs)
Recommendation: Consolidate into single Client_Document class."
example.bad: "There are some duplicate classes that should be consolidated."

principle.name: Balance Depth with Practicality
principle.description: Provide thorough analysis while respecting time constraints. Focus on high-impact findings rather than exhaustive detail on every minor issue.
principle.rationale: Users need actionable insights, not overwhelming detail. Prioritize findings that will drive the most value.

principle.name: Consider Business Context
principle.description: Technical findings should be framed in business terms. Explain the impact of issues on users, processes, and organizational goals.
principle.rationale: Stakeholders need to understand why issues matter and how fixes will benefit the organization to prioritize and fund improvements.

practice.guideline: Always start with list_root_classes to understand available class types
Use determine_class with broad keywords to discover all classes
Get complete property descriptions for each class using get_class_property_descriptions
Count documents per class to assess usage
Document the class hierarchy and inheritance relationships
Categorize properties into system, business, and integration types
Note data types, cardinality, and searchability for each property
Identify properties with unclear purposes or missing descriptions
Look for naming inconsistencies across similar properties
Use get_searchable_property_descriptions to understand search capabilities
Start with root folders and work down the hierarchy
Document folder naming conventions and patterns
Note folder depth and organization strategies
Identify empty folders or orphaned structures
Sample folder contents to verify organization effectiveness
Sample documents from different classes to verify metadata quality
Check property population rates (how many documents have values)
Verify classification accuracy by examining document content
Look for patterns in document types and categories
Assess version management practices

guideline.name: Identify Naming Convention Patterns
guideline.description: Look for prefixes, suffixes, and naming patterns that indicate: - Organizational structure (HR_, FIN_, LEG_) - User-specific classes (usr1_, usr2_) - Integration points (SAP, SF, wel) - Demo/test classes (Demo, Test, Sample)
guideline.action: Group classes by pattern and assess whether the pattern serves a valid purpose or indicates technical debt.

guideline.name: Identify Duplicate or Overlapping Classes
guideline.description: Look for classes that: - Have similar names (Invoice, Invoice_ach, JKJInvoice) - Serve similar purposes with slight variations - Have overlapping property sets - Could be consolidated with a Type or Category property
guideline.action: Document each set of duplicates with document counts and recommend consolidation strategies.

guideline.name: Assess Class and Property Usage
guideline.description: Classify classes and properties by usage level: - Active (more than 100 documents, regular use) - Moderate (10-100 documents, occasional use) - Minimal (less than 10 documents, rare use) - Unused (0 documents)
guideline.action: Recommend keeping active classes, reviewing moderate classes, and deprecating minimal/unused classes.

guideline.name: Identify Governance Gaps
guideline.description: Look for indicators of weak governance: - Classes without clear descriptions - Inconsistent naming conventions - Demo/test classes in production - User-specific classes (should be generic) - Properties without descriptions - Unclear ownership
guideline.action: Recommend governance improvements including naming standards, documentation requirements, and approval processes.

guideline.name: Document Integration Points
guideline.description: Identify and document all integration properties: - SAP integration (SAP* properties) - Salesforce integration (SF* properties) - DocuFlow integration (docuflow* properties) - Custom integrations
guideline.action: Document which classes use each integration, property naming patterns, and recommend standardization if inconsistent.

standard.requirement: Use clear hierarchical structure with numbered sections
Include table of contents for reports >5 pages
Use consistent heading levels (# for main sections, ## for subsections)
Include executive summary at the beginning
Place detailed data in appendices or separate files
Start each section with a brief overview
Use tables for structured data (class lists, property matrices)
Include specific examples for each finding
Provide quantitative metrics where possible (counts, percentages)
End each section with key takeaways or recommendations
Prioritize recommendations (High, Medium, Low)
Estimate effort level (Quick win, Short-term, Long-term)
Describe expected benefits for each recommendation
Provide specific action steps, not just general suggestions
Consider dependencies between recommendations
Use markdown formatting consistently
Use code blocks for class/property names: `ClassName`
Use tables for comparative data
Use bullet points for lists
Use bold for emphasis on key findings

pitfall.description: Analyzing too few classes to identify patterns
pitfall.why_problematic: Patterns and issues only become apparent when analyzing multiple classes. Looking at 1-2 classes won't reveal systemic issues.
pitfall.correct_approach: Analyze all classes. This provides sufficient data to identify patterns and make valid recommendations.

pitfall.description: Focusing only on technical details without business context
pitfall.why_problematic: Technical findings without business impact don't drive action. Stakeholders need to understand why issues matter.
pitfall.correct_approach: For each finding, explain the business impact: - How does this affect users? - What processes are impacted? - What are the costs (time, money, risk)? - What benefits come from fixing it?

pitfall.why_problematic: Recommendations like "improve governance" or "clean up classes" don't provide actionable guidance.
pitfall.correct_approach: Provide specific, actionable recommendations: - "Consolidate Invoice, Invoice_ach, and JKJInvoice into Finance_Invoice class" - "Remove 5 demo classes: DemoDocument, TestDoc, SampleClass, etc." - "Establish naming convention: [Domain]_[Type]_[Variant]"

pitfall.description: Overwhelming users with too much detail
pitfall.why_problematic: Extremely detailed reports with every property of every class are hard to digest and don't highlight what's important.
pitfall.correct_approach: Use a layered approach: - Executive summary with key findings - Main report with analysis and recommendations - Appendices with detailed data - Separate CSV/JSON files for complete inventories

pitfall.description: Not validating findings with document samples
pitfall.why_problematic: Class definitions don't always reflect actual usage. Documents might be misclassified or have incomplete metadata.
pitfall.correct_approach: Sample actual documents to verify: - Classification accuracy - Property population rates - Metadata quality - Content alignment with class purpose

category.name: before_starting
category.item: Confirm audit scope and objectives with user
Verify MCP server connection is working

category.name: during_audit
category.item: Use concrete examples for all findings
Sample documents to verify metadata quality
Note both issues and positive practices

category.name: before_completion
category.item: Ensure executive summary captures key points
Validate that metrics and counts are accurate
Confirm report structure is clear and logical

tip.title: Batch Similar Operations
tip.description: When analyzing multiple classes, batch similar operations together. For example, get property descriptions for 3-5 classes, then analyze them all, rather than fully analyzing one class before moving to the next.

tip.title: Use Search Strategically
tip.description: Use document_search with empty search_properties to get document counts quickly. Only do detailed document analysis on representative samples, not every document.

tip.title: Focus on High-Impact Areas
tip.description: If time is limited, focus on: 1. Classes with the most documents (highest impact) 2. Classes with obvious issues (duplicates, poor naming) 3. Integration points (often critical) 4. Classes without documentation (governance gaps)

tip.title: Leverage Patterns
tip.description: Once you identify a pattern (e.g., user-specific classes), you don't need to analyze every instance in detail. Document the pattern and list all instances.
